course_name: Popular Literature and Culture of South Asia

evaluation_components.name: Continuous Evaluations
evaluation_components.notes: Three in-class essays (20%) + one post-midsem assignment (20%). Two best essays count.

evaluation_components.name: Mid Semester
evaluation_components.notes: As per AUGSD schedule. Open book.

evaluation_components.name: Comprehensive
evaluation_components.notes: Open book. 3 hours. Covers entire course. High-value: 40% of grade.

topics.name: Introduction to South Asia
key_concepts: Culture as Process (not a fixed artifact but dynamic, contested)
Borders & Territoriality (colonial cartography, porous borders)
Margin-Centre Framework (who speaks, who is heard, from where)
Transnationalism & Globalization (texts travel; meaning shifts)
Diaspora (identity in displacement)
Gendering South Asia (gender as lens across all units)
Violence & Conflict (structural and interpersonal)
Censorship (what is suppressed and why — Manto trials, Taliban erasure)
Emergent South Asia (Bangladesh tiger, Bhutan GNH, Nepal democracy)

topics.name: Afghanistan
key_concepts: Osama — gender: erasure of public space for women under Taliban; performing mandated roles; power and surveillance; public/private binary collapse; agency under constraint

Kite Runner — framing and reception: what stories gain global acceptance; constructing 'South Asia' for Western audience; universal vs local; 'Afghanistan meets the Amazon' (Western reading practices)
Socio-political history of Afghanistan: Soviet invasion → civil war → Taliban → post-9/11
Who tells the story? — narrative legitimacy, whose voice is amplified

topics.name: Pakistan
key_concepts: Manto: partition as backdrop (not explicit political history but human cost); art of character writing (marginalized — sex workers, criminals, the mad); center-margin inversion; obscenity trials as productive censorship (what the state finds threatening reveals power structure)

Khamosh Pani: gendering the partition (women as victims/survivors/sites of honor); partition as CONTINUED event (not a historical closure); silence as stylistic and political device; reading silence (Urvashi Butalia's work on women's voices)
Reluctant Fundamentalist: post-9/11 identity; Pakistan in Western gaze; hybrid identity and its collapse under geopolitical pressure
Center-margin: colonial power, state power, narrative power

topics.name: Bangladesh and Nepal
key_concepts: Bangladesh: Rana Plaza Collapse (2013) — global labour networks; garment industry as North-South exploitation; critique of technocratic/CSR solutions; dignity of labour; culture as process (labouring bodies, fashion industry)
Bangladesh as 'emerging South Asian tiger' — development and its discontents
Nepal — 'Hands': dream narrative structure; intergenerational conflict as political allegory; symbolism of hands (labour, craft, generation); Nepal's marginality in South Asian discourse
Canon formation: what counts as literature; whose culture is studied

topics.name: Bhutan
textbook_refs: The Circle of Karma - Kunzang Choden
key_concepts: Circle of Karma: memory-based narrative structure; body as site of labour and suffering; spirituality vs materiality tension
Karma as structuring principle: culturally embedded epistemology (a way of knowing AND organizing experience)
Karma's productive AND limiting functions — not just fatalism but active ethical framework
Buddhism in contemporary Bhutan: cultural/social context; gender roles within Buddhist framework
Bhutan's uniqueness in South Asia: GNH (Gross National Happiness) ideology; isolation and modernization

topics.name: Sri Lanka
key_concepts: Funny Boy: Sri Lankan conflict (Sinhalese-Tamil, 1983 riots) as backdrop
Double exclusion: ethnic minority (Tamil) + sexuality (queer) — intersectionality of marginalization
Intimate geopolitics: how macro-political conflict shapes micro-domestic/personal spaces; everyday reproduction of conflict
Minority discourse: how minorities negotiate identity, belonging, violence
Queer identity in South Asian context — silence, naming, coming-of-age

topics.name: India
key_concepts: Neoliberal framework: promise of meritocracy vs reality of structural inequality; India shining vs India struggling
Narrative structure: epistolary (letters to Chinese premier) — who is the audience? why does Balram need to explain himself?
Spatial structure: Light (city/Gurgaon/freedom) vs Darkness (village/servitude); Balram's transgression between both
The Rooster Coop: why the poor don't rebel — internalized servitude, complicity, family as hostage
Balram as white tiger AND entrepreneur: exceptional individual vs structural change; does his success critique or affirm neoliberalism?
Slumdog Millionaire: Western gaze on Indian poverty (poverty porn debate); game show as metaphor for luck/fate/capitalism

topics.name: Comparative Analysis
key_concepts: South Asia as a region: unity in diversity — common colonial history, shared cultural flows, yet distinct national trajectories

Cross-cutting themes: gender/gendered violence (Osama, Khamosh Pani, Circle of Karma, Funny Boy); partition and its afterlives (Manto, Khamosh Pani); labour exploitation (Bangladesh, White Tiger, Slumdog); minority voices (Funny Boy, Manto's characters, Nepal's marginality)
Margin-centre across all texts: who is centered, who is marginal, and why
Culture as process: none of these texts presents culture as static — all show culture being contested, negotiated, performed
Western reception of South Asian texts: Kite Runner, Slumdog — what gets circulated and why

syllabus_exam_mismatches.note: Theoretical frameworks from introductory lectures (culture as process, margin-centre, diaspora) recur as analytical tools across ALL units — high exam value despite low lecture count
Final 3 lectures explicitly on comparative analysis; comprehensive exam likely has a comparative question — prioritize in last 2 study hours
4 lectures only; Circle of Karma is niche — likely one question max; do not over-invest relative to Pakistan/India/Afghanistan
